turns.message_text:
Welcome. My name is Solomon, and I'll be supporting your mediation process today.

Before we begin, I want to clarify my role and how this session will work. I'm here as a neutral process facilitator — not a judge, lawyer, or therapist. I don't make decisions for you or tell you what's fair. My job is to help you both communicate effectively, understand each other's concerns, explore options together, and work toward agreements that make sense for your situation.

A few ground rules:

• This is a voluntary process. Either of you can pause or end the session at any time.
• I ask that you speak respectfully, even when discussing difficult topics.
• Try to listen to understand, not just to respond.
• If at any point you need a break, just let me know.

Important: I'm designed to recognize when issues go beyond what mediation can safely handle. If I detect safety concerns, legal complexities that require attorney guidance, or other situations outside my scope, I'll let you know and we'll discuss next steps.

Now, to begin: I'd like to hear from each of you about what brought you here today. What are the main issues or decisions you're hoping to work through in this mediation?

Who would like to start?

response_synthesis.message_text:
Welcome. My name is Solomon, and I'll be supporting your mediation process today.

Before we begin, I want to clarify my role and how this session will work. I'm here as a neutral process facilitator — not a judge, lawyer, or therapist. I don't make decisions for you or tell you what's fair. My job is to help you both communicate effectively, understand each other's concerns, explore options together, and work toward agreements that make sense for your situation.

A few ground rules:

• This is a voluntary process. Either of you can pause or end the session at any time.
• I ask that you speak respectfully, even when discussing difficult topics.
• Try to listen to understand, not just to respond.
• If at any point you need a break, just let me know.

Important: I'm designed to recognize when issues go beyond what mediation can safely handle. If I detect safety concerns, legal complexities that require attorney guidance, or other situations outside my scope, I'll let you know and we'll discuss next steps.

Now, to begin: I'd like to hear from each of you about what brought you here today. What are the main issues or decisions you're hoping to work through in this mediation?

Who would like to start?

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Turn 1 is the session framing phase. No substantive issues have been presented, no party interests have been articulated, and no dispute content has emerged. The mediator is establishing ground rules and process orientation. There is no domain content to assess for option work at this stage. This is not a safety concern or information gap — it is a fundamental structural constraint of the mediation process: option work cannot occur before parties have presented the issues they need to resolve.

turns.message_text:
Thank you for bringing that concern forward. I'm hearing that the timing and documentation around reimbursement requests has been creating difficulty for you — specifically requests that arrive weeks after the expense without receipts or advance notice. That's helpful context.

Before we work on what might address this, I'd like to hear from Party B. What's your perspective on the reimbursement process that Party A just described? And then I'll want to understand from both of you what these reimbursement requests typically involve and what you're each trying to accomplish when these requests come up.

Party B, would you like to respond to what you just heard?

response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you for bringing that concern forward. I'm hearing that the timing and documentation around reimbursement requests has been creating difficulty for you — specifically requests that arrive weeks after the expense without receipts or advance notice. That's helpful context.

Before we work on what might address this, I'd like to hear from Party B. What's your perspective on the reimbursement process that Party A just described? And then I'll want to understand from both of you what these reimbursement requests typically involve and what you're each trying to accomplish when these requests come up.

Party B, would you like to respond to what you just heard?
response_synthesis.confidence_note: High confidence that inviting Party B is the correct process move. Moderate confidence in Party A interest assessment — the complaint is clear but affirmative interests remain to be drawn out.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Only one party has spoken substantively (Party A in T2). Party B has not yet articulated any interests, concerns, or response to Party A's complaint. The domain cannot assess structural fit without understanding whether Party B contests the characterization, has workability concerns, or accepts the underlying premise. Additionally, Party A's statement identifies a complaint pattern but has not yet articulated what would constitute acceptable resolution (notice windows? documentation standards? different approval structure?). This is a fundamental information absence, not a parameter-calibration gap.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Party B has not yet been heard
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Party B responds to Party A's concern — either acknowledging the pattern, disputing it, offering context, or stating their own process concerns. Even a simple acknowledgment ('I understand the timing has been frustrating') would provide sufficient basis to assess structural fit.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Party A's interest articulation is complaint-only
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Party A clarifies what acceptable process would look like (advance notice period, documentation expectations, approval thresholds) OR the mediator elicits this through follow-up questioning. Without this, we cannot distinguish between different structural solutions (e.g., tiered notice vs. receipt standards vs. advance approval).

material_gaps.gap: Nature of 'reimbursement requests' — are these shared child expenses under an existing agreement, discretionary spending by one parent, or something else?
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Understanding whether this is expense-coordination (requiring process options) vs. financial-control dispute (requiring substantive negotiation about scope of reimbursable expenses).

material_gaps.gap: Party B's perspective on the timing and documentation pattern Party A described
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Assessing whether proposed solutions need to address contested facts, practical barriers Party B faces, or simple process alignment.

material_gaps.gap: Whether there is an existing agreement governing these requests
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Determining whether options should be framed as 'establishing new protocol' vs. 'clarifying existing terms' vs. 'modifying agreement that isn't working'.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This is appropriately early in the session. Turn 2 was Party A's opening statement of the issue. The natural next step is to hear Party B's response before assessing structural options. The blocking constraint is genuine — not over-caution. In divorce mediation, attempting to qualify process options after hearing only one party's complaint (without response or interest articulation from either party) would be premature domain work. The mediator should elicit Party B's perspective and explore both parties' interests more fully. Once both parties have spoken and at least one has articulated a constructive interest (or the mediator has helped translate complaints into process needs), structural process options will become qualifiable. Expected resolution: next 1-2 turns.

positions_structured.statement: Expense coordination should stay fair and workable rather than turning into one-sided approval power.

turns.confidence_note: Deferring option work appropriately. Focusing on information gathering and interest clarification to build foundation for later option generation.
turns.message_text:
Thank you both. I'm hearing two perspectives that actually share something important — you both want a reimbursement process that works reliably.

Parent A, you're naming the difficulty when requests arrive unexpectedly, without documentation or advance notice. That uncertainty makes financial planning harder.

Parent B, you're concerned that a workable system shouldn't give either parent unilateral control over what counts as legitimate — that approval shouldn't become a barrier to meeting your child's real needs.

Before we explore solutions, I'd like to understand your current arrangement better. Right now, what expenses do you consider 'shared' that require reimbursement — as opposed to expenses that are just covered by whoever incurs them? And when a reimbursement request does come up, what usually happens? Help me understand the pattern you're both working with now.
interaction_observations_delta: [PERCEPTION:PARTY_A:INTERESTS] predictability and advance notice in financial planning; documentation standards to avoid disputes; feeling respected in co-parenting communication
[PERCEPTION:PARTY_B:INTERESTS] maintaining autonomy in decision-making about child expenses; avoiding unilateral control by other party; fairness in expense approval process

[DOMAIN:GAPS] What expenses are currently considered 'shared' vs. included in base support?; What documentation standard do they currently follow (if any)?; What timeline expectations currently exist?

maintaining some control over shared financial decisions

focus on procedural failures may mask deeper trust concerns

inferred_interests: maintaining autonomy in decision-making about child expenses
avoiding unilateral control by other party
being trusted as a competent co-parent

may feel judged or micromanaged in parenting decisions

Both remain engaged and articulate - no incapacity signals

key_constraints: Child support modification requires court approval or written agreement
Shared expenses need clear definition to be enforceable

Timeline standards for requests and reimbursements must be mutual
